report_name: TeamPCP's Sophisticated Supply Chain Attack on Trivy and LiteLLM Hits 1,000+ SaaS Environments

threat_actors.name: TeamPCP
threat_actors.description: A sophisticated threat actor group responsible for a widespread supply chain attack targeting open-source developer tools like Trivy, Checkmarx KICS, and LiteLLM. The group used a stolen GitHub token to poison CI/CD pipelines and distribute credential-stealing malware, compromising over 1,000 SaaS environments. Known for aggressive tactics and collaboration with extortion groups.
threat_actors.goals: Harvesting cloud credentials (AWS, GCP, Azure), SSH keys, Kubernetes tokens, and cryptocurrency wallets from CI/CD environments for financial gain and extortion.

threat_actors.name: Lapsus$
threat_actors.description: An extortion group mentioned as a collaborator with TeamPCP.

attack_patterns.name: Valid Accounts
attack_patterns.description: The attackers gained initial access by using a stolen GitHub Personal Access Token (PAT) from a previously incompletely remediated security incident.

attack_patterns.name: Compromise Software Dependencies and Development Tools
attack_patterns.description: TeamPCP compromised the software supply chain by force-pushing 76 malicious tags in the `aquasecurity/trivy-action` repository, a technique known as tag poisoning. This redirected CI/CD pipelines to execute attacker-controlled code.

attack_patterns.name: Unsecured Credentials
attack_patterns.description: The primary goal of the infostealer payload was to harvest cloud credentials (AWS, GCP, Azure), SSH keys, and other secrets stored in environment variables within CI/CD runners.

attack_patterns.name: Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol
attack_patterns.description: Stolen credentials and data were exfiltrated from compromised CI/CD environments to the attacker-controlled domain scan.aquasecurtiy.org.

attack_patterns.name: Exfiltration to Code Repository
attack_patterns.description: As a fallback exfiltration method, the malware was designed to upload stolen data as a release file to a newly created public GitHub repository within the victim's own account.

attack_patterns.name: Compromise Host Software Binary
attack_patterns.description: The attackers published a trojanized version of the Trivy scanner (v0.69.4) via official GitHub Releases and container registries, replacing the legitimate binary with a malicious one.

malware.name: TeamPCP Infostealer
malware.description: A potent infostealer payload injected into compromised software releases. It was designed to harvest environment variables, SSH keys, cloud credentials (AWS, GCP, Azure), Kubernetes tokens, and cryptocurrency wallets from CI/CD runners and exfiltrate them to an attacker-controlled domain.

tools.name: Trivy
tools.description: A popular open-source security scanner from Aqua Security that was compromised in the supply chain attack. Attackers distributed a trojanized version (v0.69.4) and poisoned the Git tags of its associated GitHub Actions.

tools.name: Checkmarx KICS
tools.description: A legitimate developer security tool from Checkmarx that was also compromised by TeamPCP as part of the expanding supply chain attack on March 23, 2026.

tools.name: LiteLLM
tools.description: A legitimate Python library whose PyPI packages were compromised on March 24, 2026. Malicious versions 1.82.7 and 1.82.8 were published, containing the infostealer payload.

vulnerabilities.name: CVE-2026-33634
vulnerabilities.description: This CVE identifier was assigned to the multi-stage supply chain attack campaign conducted by TeamPCP, which involved compromising multiple open-source projects through tag poisoning and malicious binary distribution to steal credentials.
vulnerabilities.kev_required_action: Apply mitigations per vendor instructions, follow applicable BOD 22-01 guidance for cloud services, or discontinue use of the product if mitigations are unavailable.
vulnerabilities.cisa_vuln_name: Aquasecurity Trivy Embedded Malicious Code Vulnerability

identities.name: Aqua Security
identities.description: The vendor of the Trivy scanner, whose GitHub repository and release pipeline were compromised in the supply chain attack.

identities.name: Checkmarx
identities.description: The vendor of the KICS and AST tools, which were also targeted and compromised by TeamPCP.

identities.name: LiteLLM Project
identities.description: The open-source project maintaining the LiteLLM Python library, whose PyPI packages were compromised with malicious code.

identities.name: SaaS Environments
identities.description: A class of over 1,000 victims whose SaaS environments were compromised due to the use of the trojanized developer tools in their CI/CD pipelines.

infrastructure.name: TeamPCP C2 and Exfil Domain
infrastructure.description: An attacker-controlled domain used for command and control and to receive stolen credentials exfiltrated from compromised CI/CD environments. The domain name misspells 'security' to appear legitimate.

infrastructure.name: LiteLLM Compromise Infrastructure
infrastructure.description: An attacker-controlled domain associated with the compromise of the LiteLLM Python library.

infrastructure.name: TeamPCP Malicious Code Repository
infrastructure.description: GitHub repositories named 'tpcp-docs' used by the threat actor to host malicious code or tools as part of the attack campaign.

indicators.name: TeamPCP Supply Chain Attack C2/Exfil Domains
indicators.x_netsecio_sigma_rule:
title: TeamPCP Supply Chain Attack C2 DNS Query
id: 2b8c1f9a-5d7e-4b0c-8a1d-9f6e3c2b1a0e
status: experimental
description: Detects DNS queries to domains associated with the TeamPCP supply chain attack on Trivy and LiteLLM.
references:
  - https://www.paloaltonetworks.com/blog/2026/03/trivy-supply-chain-attack/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-03-25
tags:
  - attack.t1071.004
  - attack.command_and_control
falsepositives:
  - None expected.
level: critical
logsource:
  category: dns_query
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    QueryName|contains:
      - 'scan.aquasecurtiy.org'
      - 'models.litellm.cloud'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects network communication to known command and control (C2) and data exfiltration domains used by TeamPCP in the Trivy/LiteLLM supply chain attack.

indicators.name: Suspicious Git Force Push
indicators.pattern:
title: Suspicious Git Force Push Command
id: 8d3e4f5a-1b2c-4d5e-8f9a-0b1c2d3e4f5a
status: experimental
description: Detects the use of 'git push --force', which can be indicative of tag poisoning or other malicious repository manipulation as seen in the TeamPCP supply chain attack.
references:
  - https://www.paloaltonetworks.com/blog/2026/03/trivy-supply-chain-attack/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-03-25
tags:
  - attack.t1195.001
  - attack.initial_access
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate force pushes by developers during feature development, though this is often against best practice for shared branches.
level: medium
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    Image|endswith: '\git.exe'
    CommandLine|contains|all:
      - 'push'
      - '--force'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects the execution of `git push --force` commands, a technique used by TeamPCP to overwrite legitimate Git tags with malicious versions (tag poisoning).

indicators.name: Anomalous Egress from CI/CD Runner
indicators.pattern:
title: Anomalous Egress Traffic from CI/CD Runner
id: c7a1b2d3-e4f5-4a6b-7c8d-9e0f1a2b3c4d
status: experimental
description: Detects outbound network traffic from a CI/CD runner to a domain not on a pre-approved allowlist. This was the primary exfiltration method in the TeamPCP attack.
references:
  - https://www.paloaltonetworks.com/blog/2026/03/trivy-supply-chain-attack/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-03-25
tags:
  - attack.t1048
  - attack.exfiltration
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate connections to new or unlisted services.
level: high
logsource:
  category: network_connection
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    Initiated: 'true'
    # This field assumes enrichment where the source process is identified as a CI/CD agent
    Image|contains:
      - 'runner'
      - 'agent'
      - 'build'
  filter:
    DestinationHostname|endswith:
      - '.github.com'
      - '.docker.io'
      - '.pkg.dev'
  condition: selection and not filter
indicators.description: Detects egress network traffic from CI/CD runners to non-standard or uncategorized domains, which could indicate data exfiltration to attacker infrastructure like scan.aquasecurtiy.org.
